params.text:
Write facts, not opinions

Objective recording means describing what you actually saw, heard or did — not what you assumed or felt. Opinion and loose labels can mislead colleagues and offend the person if they read their own notes.

  • Opinion: “Mrs Ali was being difficult and aggressive today.”
  • Fact: “Mrs Ali declined her lunch, raised her voice and said she wanted to be left alone. She accepted a drink 20 minutes later.”

Where you do record the person's view or a professional judgement, make clear whose view it is — ideally in the person's own words. Never guess, and never record something you did not witness as if you did.

params.question: Which daily-note entry is written correctly — factual, objective and person-centred?

answers.text: "Refused all care and was aggressive. Attention-seeking as usual."

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is opinion and judgement, not fact. Labels like "attention-seeking" are disrespectful and unhelpful.

answers.text: "Declined personal care this morning, saying she felt too tired. Offered again after lunch and she agreed. Washed and dressed with support at 1:30pm."

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. It records what happened and when, in the person's terms, without judgement.

answers.text: "Seemed a bit off. Probably not eating enough. Will keep an eye."

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is vague and speculative. Record what you actually observed, with specifics.
Too vague to be useful. Even a settled day should note what care was given and any observations.
